--- SOURCE 4: Complex system ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system

SUMMARY:
| Complex systems | As an interdisciplinary domain, complex systems draw contributions from many different fields, such as the study of self-organization and critical phenomena from physics, of spontaneous order from the social sciences, chaos from mathematics, adaptation from biology, and many others. _Complex systems_ is therefore often used as a broad term encompassing a research approach to problems in many diverse disciplines, including statistical physics, information theory, nonlinear dynamics, anthropology, computer science, meteorology, sociology, economics, psychology, and biology. Complex systems can be: *   Stefan Thurner, Peter Klimek, Rudolf Hanel: _Introduction to the Theory of Complex Systems_, Oxford University Press, 2018, ISBN "ISBN (identifier)")978-0198821939 "Complex Systems". *   Complex Systems Modeling based on Luis M. *   Complex systems theory

| * [v](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Template:Complex_systems "Template:Complex systems") * [t](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Template_talk:Complex_systems "Template talk:Complex systems") * [e](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Complex_systems "Special:EditPage/Template:Complex systems") |

A **complex system** is a [system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/System "System") composed of many components that may interact with one another.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-1) Examples of complex systems are Earth's global [climate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate "Climate"), [organisms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organisms "Organisms"), the [human brain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain"), infrastructure such as power grid, transportation or communication systems, complex [software](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software "Software") and electronic systems, social and economic organizations (like [cities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cities "Cities")), an [ecosystem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem"), a living [cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)"), and, ultimately, for some authors, the entire [universe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universe "Universe").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-3)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-4)

The behavior of a complex system is intrinsically difficult to model due to the dependencies, competitions, relationships, and other types of interactions between their parts or between a given system and its environment.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-5) Systems that are "[complex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complexity "Complexity")" have distinct properties that arise from these relationships, such as [nonlinearity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonlinear_system "Nonlinear system"), [emergence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emergence "Emergence"), [spontaneous order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spontaneous_order "Spontaneous order"), [adaptation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_adaptive_system "Complex adaptive system"), and [feedback loops](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feedback "Feedback"), among others.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-6) Because such systems appear in a wide variety of fields, the commonalities among them have become the topic of their independent area of research. In many cases, it is useful to represent such a system as a network where the nodes represent the components and links represent their interactions.

The term _complex systems_ often refers to the study of complex systems, which is an approach to science that investigates how relationships between a system's parts give rise to its collective behaviors and how the system interacts and forms relationships with its environment.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-7) The study of complex systems regards collective, or system-wide, behaviors as the fundamental object of study; for this reason, complex systems can be understood as an alternative paradigm to [reductionism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reductionism "Reductionism"), which attempts to explain systems in terms of their constituent parts and the individual interactions between them.

As an interdisciplinary domain, complex systems draw contributions from many different fields, such as the study of [self-organization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self-organization "Self-organization") and critical phenomena from physics, of [spontaneous order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spontaneous_order "Spontaneous order") from the social sciences, [chaos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaos_theory "Chaos theory") from mathematics, [adaptation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_adaptive_system "Complex adaptive system") from biology, and many others. _Complex systems_ is therefore often used as a broad term encompassing a research approach to problems in many diverse disciplines, including [statistical physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Statistical_physics "Statistical physics"), [information theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Information_theory "Information theory"), [nonlinear dynamics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonlinear_system "Nonlinear system"), [anthropology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology"), [computer science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), [meteorology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology"), [sociology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sociology "Sociology"), [economics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economics "Economics"), [psychology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychology "Psychology"), and [biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biology "Biology").

Types of systems
----------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Complex_system&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types of systems")]

Complex systems can be:

*   [**Complex adaptive systems**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_adaptive_system "Complex adaptive system") which have the capacity to change.
*   **Polycentric systems:** “where many elements are capable of making mutual adjustments for ordering their relationships with one another within a general system of rules where each element acts with independence of other elements”.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-8)
*   **Disorganised systems** involving localized interactions of multiple entities that do not form a coherent whole.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-9) Disorganised systems are linked to [self-organisation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self-organization "Self-organization") processes.
*   **Hierarchic systems** which are analyzable into successive sets of subsystems.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-10) They can also be called nested or embedded systems.
*   **[Cybernetic systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cybernetic_system "Cybernetic system")** involve information feedback loops.

Key concepts
------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Complex_system&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Key concepts")]

### Adaptation

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Complex_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Adaptation")]

[Complex adaptive systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_adaptive_system "Complex adaptive system") are special cases of complex systems that are [adaptive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adaptive_system "Adaptive system") in that they have the capacity to change and learn from experience.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-11) Examples of complex adaptive systems include the international [trade](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trade "Trade") markets, social insect and [ant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ant "Ant") colonies, the [biosphere](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biosphere "Biosphere") and the [ecosystem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem"), the [brain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain") and the [immune system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immune_system "Immune system"), the [cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") and the developing [embryo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embryo "Embryo"), cities, [manufacturing businesses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing") and any human social group-based endeavor in a cultural and [social system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_system "Social system") such as [political parties](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Political_party "Political party") or [communities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Community "Community").[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-12)

### Decomposability

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Complex_system&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Decomposability")]

A system is **decomposable** if the parts of the system (subsystems) are independent from each other, for exemple the model of a [perfect gas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perfect_gas "Perfect gas") consider the relations among molecules negligeable.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-:1-13)

In a **nearly decomposable** system, the interactions between subsystems are weak but not negligeable, this is often the case in social systems.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-:1-13) Conceptually, a system is nearly decomposable if the variables composing it can be separated into classes and subclasses, if these variables are independent for many functions but affect each other, and if the whole system is greater than the parts.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-14)

Features
--------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Complex_system&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Features")]

Complex systems may have the following features:[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-15)

Complex systems may be open Complex systems are usually [open systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_system_(systems_theory) "Open system (systems theory)") – that is, they exist in a [thermodynamic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermodynamic "Thermodynamic") gradient and dissipate energy. In other words, complex systems are frequently far from energetic [equilibrium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermodynamic_equilibrium "Thermodynamic equilibrium"): but despite this flux, there may be [pattern stability](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pattern_stability&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pattern stability (page does not exist)"),[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#cite_note-16) see [synergetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Synergetics_(Haken) "Synergetics (Haken)").Complex systems may exhibit critical transitions[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Alternative_stable_states%2C_critical_transitions%2C_and_the_direction_of_critical_slowing_down.png/250px-Alternative_stable_states%2C_critical_transitions%2C_and_the_direction_of_critical_slowing_down.png)](https://en.wikipedia

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 5: What is complex systems science? ---
URL: https://www.santafe.edu/what-is-complex-systems-science

SUMMARY:
The classic popular science book that introduced SFI and the sciences of complexity to the general public. A wonderful introduction to complexity research by SFI Davis Professor Melanie Mitchell, suitable for both scientists and educated non-experts. A popular description of classic complex systems phenomena by SFI External Professor and Science Board member John Miller, with a focus on how insights from complexity science could be applied in social systems and economics. A forthcoming collection of research papers that set the course for the science of complex systems. The introduction of random boolean networks by former SFI Professor Stuart Kauffman, which helped launch network-based complexity science. A complex systems view of the economy by SFI External Professor Brian Arthur.

What is complex systems science?
================================

Scientists are working across disciplines to render complex reality to scientific understanding.

It has been the great triumph of the sciences to find consistent means of studying phenomena hidden by both space and time, overcoming the limits of cognition and material culture. To hide in space means that phenomena lie beyond the scope of our everyday senses because they are either too small or too distant to be detected without amplification. Things can be hidden in time by being too fast for us to perceive or too slow for a single lifetime to encompass.

The scientific method is the portmanteau of instruments, formalisms, and experimental practices that succeed in discovering basic mechanisms despite the limitations of individual intelligence.

There are, however, on this planet, phenomena that are hidden in plain sight. These are the phenomena that we study as complex systems: the convoluted exhibitions of the adaptive world — from cells to societies. Examples of these complex systems include cities, economies, civilizations, the nervous system, the Internet,and ecosystems.

Paradoxically, the complex world is one that we can, in many senses, perceive and measure directly. Unlike distant stars or nearby minerals that require a significant increase in optical capability to arrive at insights into their elementary properties, behavior — both individual and collective — seems to present itself in ways that can be investigated rather modestly through observation or experiment.

But the way in which complex phenomena are hidden, beyond masking by space and time, is through nonlinearity, randomness, collective dynamics, hierarchy, and emergence — a deck of attributes that have proved ill-suited to our intuitive and augmented abilities to grasp and to comprehend.

Over the course of thirty-five years, the Santa Fe Institute has been looking into this proximal, near-invisible reality, working in highly diverse, nondisciplinary teams to invent new concepts to render up complex reality to science; searching for order in the complexity of evolving worlds.

—David Krakauer, President

Santa Fe Institute

Adapted from [_Worlds Hidden in Plain Sight_](https://www.sfipress.org/books/worlds-hidden-in-plain-sight)(SFI Press, 2019)

Further reading
---------------

Books and foundational papers on complexity science

#### Books

##### Less technical

[Complexity: The Emerging Science at the Edge of Order and Chaos](http://www.simonandschuster.com/books/Complexity/Mitchell-M-Waldrop/9780671872342)

_M. Mitchell Waldrop (1993)_

The classic popular science book that introduced SFI and the sciences of complexity to the general public. Still a highly enjoyable read, it profiles the people behind the science.

[Chaos: Making a New Science](https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books/321477/chaos-by-james-gleick/9780143113454/)

_J. Gleick (Penguin, 1988)_

The highly acclaimed popular science book that introduced complexity's "cousins," chaos and nonlinear dynamical systems, to the general public. It also features several scientists currently or formerly associated with SFI.

[Complexity: A Guided Tour](https://global.oup.com/academic/product/complexity-9780195124415?cc=us&lang=en&)

_M. Mitchell (Oxford University Press, 2009)_

A wonderful introduction to complexity research by SFI Davis Professor Melanie Mitchell, suitable for both scientists and educated non-experts. A very readable book that also became the[first online course in complexity,](https://www.complexityexplorer.org/courses/104-introduction-to-complexity)for SFI's online courses on[Complexity Explorer](https://www.complexityexplorer.org/courses).

[Complexity: A Very Short Introduction](https://books.google.com/books?id=lmygAwAAQBAJ&printsec=frontcover&source=gbs_ge_summary_r&cad=0#v=onepage&q&f=false)

_J.H. Holland (Oxford University Press, 2014)_

Key attributes, elements, examples, and frameworks of complexity, as described by one of the giants of the field.

[A Crude Look at the Whole: The Science of Complex Systems in Business, Life, and Society](https://www.basicbooks.com/titles/john-h-miller/a-crude-look-at-the-whole/9780465055692/)

_J.H. Miller (Basic Books, 2016)_

A popular description of classic complex systems phenomena by SFI External Professor and Science Board member John Miller, with a focus on how insights from complexity science could be applied in social systems and economics.

[Scale: The Universal Laws of Life, Growth, and Death in Organisms, Cities, and Companies](https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books/314049/scale-by-geoffrey-west/9780143110903/)

_G. West (Penguin, 2017)_

About the universal scaling laws in biology and in man-made living systems such as cities and companies. A unique and acclaimed popular science book by SFI Distinguished Shannan Professor Geoffrey West.

[Worlds Hidden in Plain Sight](https://sfipress.org/books/worlds-hidden-in-plain-sight)

_D. Krakauer (SFI Press, 2017)_

A collection of first-person essays on complex systems, authored by the scientists who have been pioneering new methods to understand them. Edited by David Krakauer, SFI President and William H. Miller Professor of Complex Systems.

##### More technical

[Exploring Complexity: An Introduction](https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/783593.Exploring_Complexity)

_G. Nicolis and I. Prigogine (W.H. Freeman, 1989)_

One of the first textbooks on complexity science, co-authored by Nobel Laureate Ilya Prigogine. Mostly written from a physics point of view (non-equilibrium and non-linear systems theory), but with clear explanations of all topics and terminology.

[Complexity: Metaphors, Models, and Reality](https://www.amazon.com/Complexity-Metaphors-Models-Reality-George/dp/0201626063)

_G. A. Cowan, D. Pines and D. Meltzer (Avalon, 1994)_

Proceedings from the first 10 years of complexity research. A rich collection of review and research papers by many of the scientists associated with SFI in the early days.

[Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos: With Applications to Physics, Biology, Chemistry, and Engineering](https://www.crcpress.com/Nonlinear-Dynamics-and-Chaos-With-Applications-to-Physics-Biology-Chemistry/Strogatz/p/book/9780813349107)

_S. H. Strogatz (Avalon, 2000)_

An introductory textbook on two central topics in complexity science, by former SFI External Professor Steven Strogatz (one of the best teachers on the topics).

[Networks](https://global.oup.com/academic/product/networks-9780198805090?cc=us&lang=en&)

_M. Newman (Oxford University Press, 2010)_

The standard textbook on everything related to networks, by SFI External Professor Mark Newman. Network science is a pillar for studying complex systems, and an offshoot of complex systems science.

[Introduction to the Theory of Complex Systems](https://global.oup.com/academic/product/introduction-to-the-theory-of-complex-systems-9780198821939?cc=us&lang=en&)

_S. Thurner, R. Hanel and P. Klimek (Oxford University Press, 2018)_

The most up-to-date textbook on complex systems, co-authored by SFI External Professor Stephan Thurner. It presents many foundational topics such as networks, scaling laws, evolution, and information theory, along with a complexity theory based on a universal statistical mechanism.

[Foundational Papers in Complexity Science](https://sfi-edu.s3.amazonaws.com/sfi-edu/production/uploads/ckeditor/2020/07/21/foundational-papers-of-complexity-science-toc.pdf)

_Santa Fe Institute (SFI Press,_ Forthcoming _)_

A forthcoming collection of research papers that set the course for the science of complex systems. Follow the link above to view the table of contents.

#### Papers

[More is Different](http://robotics.cs.tamu.edu/dshell/cs689/papers/anderson72more_is_different.pdf)

_P. W. Anderson_

_Science_ 177:393-396, 1972

A very early paper about complexity by Nobel laureate and SFI co-founder Phil Anderson. It describes broken symmetry, emergence, and the hierarchical structure of science. It set a new course for complex systems science as a departure from reductionism.

[What is Complexity?](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epdf/10.1002/cplx.6130010105)

_M. Gell-Mann

 Complexity_ 1:16-19, 1995

Remarks on simplicity and complexity by Nobel laureate and SFI co-founder Murray Gell-Mann, in the very first issue of the _Complexity_ journal.

[The Architecture of Complexity](http://www2.econ.iastate.edu/tesfatsi/ArchitectureOfComplexity.HSimon1962.pdf)

_H. A. Simon_

_Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society_ 106:467-482, 1962

Another very early paper about complexity and its hierarchical structure by Nobel laureate Herbert Simon.

[Metabolic Stability and Epigenesis in Randomly Constructed Genetic Nets](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Metabolic-stability-and-epigenesis-in-randomly-Kauffman/93e430fc351766b408c39a89c4421546d12632d0)

_S. A. Kauffman

 Journal of Theoretical Biology_ 22:437-467, 1969

The introduction of random boolean networks by former SFI Professor Stuart Kauffman, which helped launch network-based complexity science.

[The Evolution of Cooperation](http://science.sciencemag.org/content/211/4489/1390)

_R. Axelrod and W. D. Hamilton

 Science_ 211:1390-1396, 1981

A groundbreaking paper introducing computer simulations based on game theory in evolutionary biology. It provides an explanation for the evolution of cooperation among selfish individuals.

[The Evolution of Emergent Computation](http://www.pnas.org/content/92/23/10742)

_J. P. Crutchfield and M. Mitchell

 PNAS_ 92:10742-10746, 1995

An overview of the evolution of emergent computation in cellular automata, by SFI External Professor Jim Crutchfield and SFI Davis Professor Melanie Mitchell.

[Studying Artificial Life with Cellular Automata](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/016727898690237X)

_C. G. Langton

 Physica D_ 22:120-149, 1986

Former SFI faculty member Chris Langton's early work on cellular automata, which led to a new field of research in artificial life (ALife).

[Chaos](http://csc.ucdavis.edu/~chaos/courses/poci/Readings/Chaos_SciAm1986/Chaos_SciAm1986.html)

_J. P. Crutchfield, J. D. Farmer, N. H. Packard and R. S. Shaw_

_Scientific American_ 254:46-57, 1986

"There is order in chaos," posits this overview article by some of the pioneers of chaos theory, including SFI external faculty members Jim Crutchfield, Doyne Farmer, and Norman Packard.

[Punctuated Equilibrium and Criticality in a Simple Model of Evolution](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.71.4083)

_P. Bak and K. Sneppen_

_Physical Review Letters_ 71:4083-4086, 1993

The introduction of a simple model for studying self-organized criticality, which formed the basis of many later models and studies, by Per Bak and Kim Sneppen.

[Positive Feedbacks in the Economy](https://www.jstor.org/stable/24996687?seq=1)

_W. B. Arthur_

_Scientific American_, February 1990, 92-99

A complex systems view of the economy by SFI External Professor Brian Arthur.

[A General Model for the Origin of Allometric Scaling Laws in Biology](http://science.sciencemag.org/content/276/5309/122)

_G. B. West, J. H. Brown and B. J. Enquist

 Science_ 276:122-126, 1997

The groundbreaking paper by SFI's Geoffrey West, James Brown, and Brian Enquist which explains universal scaling laws (aka power laws) in biology.

[Collective Dynamics of 'Small-World’ Networks](https://www.nature.com/articles/30918)

_D. J. Watts and S. H. Strogatz

 Nature_ 393:440–442, 1998

Another groundbreaking paper introducing a model for the well-known "six degrees of separation" phenomenon in social networks, by SFI associates Duncan Watts and Steven Strogatz.

[Emergence of Scaling in Random Networks](http://science.sciencemag.org/content/286/5439/509)

_A.L. Barabasi and R. Albert

 Science_ 286:509-512, 1999

A nice overview paper on scaling in random networks, by some of the experts on the topic.

[Emergent Computation: Self-organizing, Collective, and Cooperative Phenomena in Natural and Artificial Computing Networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/016727899090063U)

_S. Forrest_

_Physica D_ 42:1-11, 1990

An introduction to the topic of emergent computation by SFI External Professor Stephanie Forrest.

[Resource Letter CS-1: Complex Systems](https://doi.org/10.1119/1.3590372)

_M. Newman_

_American Journal of Physics_ 79:800, 2011

A working definition of complex systems that catalogs quantitative progress in understanding them, by SFI External Professor Mark Newman.

[Universality and complexity in cellular phenomena](https://linkprotect.cudasvc.com/url?a=https%3a%2f%2fwww.stephenwolfram.com%2fpublications%2fcellular-automata-complexity%2f&c=E,1,4TEsZqwlRqzPAg_OuKEYHcLgmZZTLpc2aazENoCbIHrhrNXq7l974_DupdNXZ-k2yw9YBBgzLzl0acg3d6KILxtxx1bAE2_wRuBsNJ0sJKSssyZxJMX9vmc,&typo=1)

_S. Wolfram_

_Physica D_ 10:1-35, 1984

A landmark paper on cellular automata, which are in-silico models for biological evolution.

Science for a Complex World
---------------------------

--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: http://www.nwp.usace.army.mil/portals/24/siteimages/environment/estuary/Function_main.png
DESCRIPTION: None

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://cdn.britannica.com/35/153035-050-FAF59A2C/food-chain-producers-consumers-decomposers.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates a terrestrial food chain, showing the interactions between primary producers like plants, primary consumers such as small herbivorous mammals and insects, secondary consumers including small predators, and tertiary consumers like large predators, along with the role of decomposers like fungi and bacteria.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://d32ogoqmya1dw8.cloudfront.net/images/integrate/teaching_materials/food_supply/student_materials/simplified_diagram_typical_ecosyste_744.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates the complex interactions within an ecosystem, highlighting how climatic conditions, soil resources, plants, microbes, herbivores, and carnivores (including humans) are interconnected through feeding relationships and resource recycling.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://pointofview.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Example-of-Complex-System.png
DESCRIPTION: Multiple interconnected feedback loops illustrate how natural disaster impacts, such as a cascade effect, influence the availability of human health, drinking water, electricity, communication technology, relief units, and transportation infrastructure within a complex ecosystem.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://www.e-education.psu.edu/geog430/sites/www.e-education.psu.edu.geog430/files/Lesson_02/Images/Social-EcologicalSystems.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates the interconnections between local environment, local people, cultural practices, and various influencing factors, highlighting how climate, regional policies, and international considerations like climate change interact within social-ecological systems.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Ecosystems are intricate networks of organisms interacting with each other and their environment, exhibiting emergent properties like resilience and stability through complex interdependencies.
Social networks consist of interconnected individuals or groups that influence behaviors and information flow, showcasing non-linear dynamics and emergent collective behaviors within communities.
Economic systems involve multiple agents interacting in markets, where supply, demand, and external factors create complex behaviors such as market fluctuations and economic cycles.

Engineering systems comprise interconnected components designed to perform specific functions, where interactions among parts can create unexpected behaviors, influencing overall system performance and reliability.
